content.text:
## Answer

**Herdr** is a terminal-native agent runtime and multiplexer. Built in Rust, it runs real terminal processes in PTYs and adds layout, mouse control, state awareness, persistence, and a socket API around them. Designed for running multiple coding agents simultaneously.

---

## 1. What Herdr Is

Terminal workspace manager purpose-built for AI coding agents. Runs real processes in PTYs (not reconstructed chat views). Key differentiators vs tmux/Zellij/agent apps:

| Capability | Herdr |
|---|---|
| Agent state (working/blocked/done/idle) | ✅ |
| Direct agent attach | ✅ |
| Agents can orchestrate Herdr | ✅ (via CLI/socket API) |
| Mouse-native UI | ✅ |
| Persistent sessions with detach/reattach | ✅ |
| Remote attach over SSH | ✅ |
| Native agent session restore | ✅ |
| Live handoff (server replacement) | ✅ |

GitHub: `https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr`

---

## 2. Install/Setup

**Linux/macOS (stable):**
```bash
curl -fsSL https://herdr.dev/install.sh | sh
```

**Windows (preview beta):**
```powershell
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -c "irm https://herdr.dev/install.ps1 | iex"
```

**Package managers:**
```bash
brew install herdr          # Homebrew
mise use -g herdr           # mise
nix run github:ogulcancelik/herdr/v0.x.y  # Nix flake
```

**Manual:** Download binary from GitHub releases, `chmod +x`, move to `~/.local/bin/herdr`.

**Update:** `herdr update` (direct installs only). Homebrew/mise/Nix use their own managers.

**Update channels:** `herdr channel set preview|stable`. Preview = prereleases from master.

**Verify:** Run `herdr` — launches or attaches to default session.

---

## 3. CLI Commands

**Launch & status:**
```
herdr                          # launch/attach default session
herdr --session work           # named session
herdr --remote workbox         # SSH remote attach
herdr --no-session             # single-process escape hatch
herdr --version
herdr update [--handoff]
herdr status [server|client]
```

**Sessions:**
```
herdr session list [--json]
herdr session attach <name>
herdr session stop <name>
herdr session delete <name>
```

**Workspaces:**
```
herdr workspace list
herdr workspace create [--cwd PATH] [--label TEXT] [--focus]
herdr workspace focus <id>
herdr workspace rename <id> <label>
herdr workspace close <id>
```

**Tabs:**
```
herdr tab list [--workspace ID]
herdr tab create [--cwd PATH] [--label TEXT]
herdr tab focus <id> | rename <id> <label> | close <id>
```

**Panes:**
```
herdr pane list | get <id> | close <id>
herdr pane split <id> --direction right|down [--ratio FLOAT]
herdr pane focus --direction left|right|up|down
herdr pane resize --direction ... [--amount FLOAT]
herdr pane zoom [<id>] [--toggle|--on|--off]
herdr pane read <id> [--source visible|recent|recent-unwrapped|detection] [--lines N]
herdr pane send-text <id> <text>
herdr pane send-keys <id> <key> [key...]
herdr pane run <id> <command>     # atomic text+Enter
herdr pane rename <id> <label>
```

**Agents:**
```
herdr agent list | get <target> | focus <target>
herdr agent read <target> [--source visible|recent|detection]
herdr agent send <target> <text>
herdr agent rename <target> <name>
herdr agent wait <target> --status <idle|working|blocked|done> [--timeout MS]
herdr agent attach <target> [--takeover]
herdr agent start <name> [--cwd PATH] -- <argv...>
herdr agent explain <target> [--json|--verbose]
```

**Integrations:**
```
herdr integration install pi|omp|claude|codex|copilot|droid|kimi|opencode|kilo|hermes|qodercli|cursor
herdr integration uninstall <agent>
herdr integration status [--outdated-only]
```

**Waits:**
```
herdr wait output <pane_id> --match <text> [--regex] [--timeout MS]
herdr wait agent-status <pane_id> --status <state> [--timeout MS]
```

**Worktrees (Git checkout provenance):**
```
herdr worktree list | create | open | remove
```

---

## 4. Session/Process Management

**Architecture:** Client/server split. Server owns panes and process state. Client is the terminal UI.

```
ctrl+b q       → detach client (server + agents keep running)
herdr          → reattach
herdr server stop → kills server and all pane processes
```

**Four state persistence paths:**

| Path | Processes survive? | Layout returns? | Screen returns? | Agent conversation resumes? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Detach/reattach** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (live terminal) | ✅ (process never stopped) |
| **Server restart (snapshot restore)** | ❌ | ✅ | Only with pane_history | Only with native agent restore |
| **Update w/o --handoff** | compat servers keep running | ✅ | Only with pane_history | Only with native agent restore |
| **Update with --handoff** | best effort | ✅ | ✅ (if handoff succeeds) | ✅ (if handoff succeeds) |

**Named sessions:** Separate server namespaces with own sockets, panes, state. Use when full isolation needed:
```bash
herdr session attach work
herdr session attach side-project
```

**Agent state model:** `working` → `blocked` (needs input) → `done` (unseen) → `idle` (seen). Sidebar rolls state upward: blocked agent → blocked pane → blocked tab → blocked workspace.

**Remote attach:** `herdr --remote workbox` — local thin client connects via SSH, streams UI back. Supports Linux/macOS hosts on x86_64/aarch64.

---

## 5. Configuration

Config lives at `~/.config/herdr/`. Key sections:

**UI:**
```toml
[ui]
sidebar_width = 32
sidebar_min_width = 18
sidebar_max_width = 36
mouse_capture = true
right_click_passthrough_modifier = ""  # set "ctrl" for mouse-reporting apps
redraw_on_focus_gained = true
mouse_scroll_lines = 3
confirm_close = true
agent_panel_scope = "all"              # or "current"
accent = "cyan"
```

**Notifications (toast):**
```toml
[ui.toast]
delivery = "off"        # "off" | "herdr" | "terminal" | "system"
delay_seconds = 1

[ui.toast.herdr]
position = "bottom-right"
```

**Sound:**
```toml
[ui.sound]
enabled = true
# done_path, request_path for custom mp3s
# Per-agent: [ui.sound.agents] droid = "off"
```

**Session/restore:**
```toml
[session]
resume_agents_on_restore = true   # native agent session restore
```

**Experimental:**
```toml
[experimental]
pane_history = false              # save pane contents across restarts
allow_nested = false              # Herdr-inside-Herdr
kitty_graphics = false            # experimental image support
reveal_hidden_cursor_for_cjk_ime = false
cjk_ime_agents = []
switch_ascii_input_source_in_prefix = false
```

**Scrollback:**
```toml
[advanced]
scrollback_limit_bytes = 10485760   # 10MB
```

**Worktrees:**
```toml
[worktrees]
directory = "~/.local/share/herdr/worktrees"
```

**Remote:**
```toml
[remote]
manage_ssh_config = true
```

**Environment variables:**
| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `HERDR_CONFIG_PATH` | Override config file path |
| `HERDR_SESSION` | Select named session for CLI commands |
| `HERDR_SOCKET_PATH` | Socket path override |
| `HERDR_LOG` | Log filter (e.g. `herdr=debug`) |
| `HERDR_DISABLE_SOUNDD` | Disable sound playback |

**Logs:** `~/.config/herdr/herdr.log`, `herdr-client.log`, `herdr-server.log` (auto-rotate).

**Socket API:** Newline-delimited JSON over Unix domain socket (`~/.config/herdr/herdr.sock`). Supports workspace/pane/agent CRUD, event subscriptions, agent state reporting, waits, worktree management, notifications.

---

## Evidence

All content sourced from `herdr.dev` documentation (fetched 2026-06-12):
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/` — Overview
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/install/` — Installation
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/quick-start/` — Quick start
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/concepts/` — Workspace/Tab/Pane/Agent/Session/Client-Server
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/agents/` — Agent detection, manifests, state authority
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/cli-reference/` — Full CLI reference
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/configuration/` — Config options
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/session-state/` — Persistence paths
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/socket-api/` — Socket API protocol
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/integrations/` — Per-agent integration details
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/persistence-remote/` — Remote/SSH access
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/agent-skill/` — Agent skill file (SKILL.md)

## Caveats

- **Windows is preview-only beta** — no stable Windows release yet
- **Native Windows `--remote` not supported** — must SSH in and run herdr there
- **Pane screen history off by default** (secrets/tokens risk)
- **Live handoff is experimental** — opt-in only, best-effort process preservation
- **Herdr can't detect agents behind tmux** — if shell auto-enters tmux inside a Herdr pane, Herdr sees tmux as the process, not the agent
- **Blocked detection strict** — new/unknown agent prompts may show as `idle` until Herdr learns the screen shape

content.text:
Need Pi integration section (got truncated) and socket API docs.## Answer

## 1. Pi Integration (`herdr integration install pi`)

**Install:** `herdr integration install pi`

**What it does:** Herdr writes a bundled TypeScript extension to:
```
~/.pi/agent/extensions/herdr-agent-state.ts
```
If `PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR` is set, it writes to `$PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR/extensions/herdr-agent-state.ts` instead. Extensions directory must pre-exist.

**Effect:** Pi integration is a **lifecycle authority** — when installed and actively reporting, it authors `idle`, `working`, and `blocked` states. Herdr does NOT also run screen manifest fallback for Pi when integration is active. It also provides **native session identity** for restore after Herdr server restart.

**Minimum version:** Pi integration version 2 for native session restore.

**Uninstall:** `herdr integration uninstall pi` — removes only the extension file.

## 2. How Herdr Detects Pi Agents

**Two detection paths:**

| Path | When active | What it provides |
|---|---|---|
| **Lifecycle hooks (integration)** | `herdr integration install pi` installed | `idle`/`working`/`blocked` + session identity |
| **Screen manifest detection** | No integration installed | Reads bottom-buffer terminal snapshot, evaluates TOML manifests |

**Status authority model (from `/docs/agents/`):**
- Pi row: *"lifecycle hooks when installed; otherwise screen manifest"* — state and session
- Screen detection reads live bottom-buffer (not scrolled viewport)
- TOML manifests match against terminal UI patterns to classify states
- Blocked detection is strict: only matches known approval/permission UI patterns
- Unknown prompts fall back to `idle` with label `default_known_agent_idle_fallback`

**Supported agents table confirms Pi gets both state authority AND session identity** — one of the richest integrations alongside Kimi, Hermes, OpenCode, and Kilo.

## 3. Socket API Protocol

**Transport:** Newline-delimited JSON over Unix domain socket.

**Socket paths (resolution order):**
1. Explicit `--session <name>`
2. `HERDR_SOCKET_PATH`
3. `HERDR_SESSION=<name>`
4. Default: `~/.config/herdr/herdr.sock`
5. Named sessions: `~/.config/herdr/sessions/<name>/herdr.sock`

**Key methods for programmatic control:**

```json
// Ping
{"id":"req_1","method":"ping","params":{}}

// Report agent state (lifecycle authority)
{"id":"req_1","method":"pane.report_agent","params":{
  "pane_id":"1-1","source":"custom:docs","agent":"docs-bot",
  "state":"working","message":"building docs","custom_status":"indexing"
}}

// Report session identity for restore
{"id":"req_2","method":"pane.report_agent_session","params":{
  "pane_id":"1-1","source":"herdr:codex","agent":"codex","agent_session_id":"..."
}}

// Display-only metadata (doesn't override semantic state)
{"id":"req_2","method":"pane.report_metadata","params":{
  "pane_id":"1-1","source":"user:claude-title","agent":"claude",
  "title":"Refactor auth","display_agent":"Claude: auth",
  "state_labels":{"working":"refactoring auth","idle":"ready","done":"review ready"},
  "ttl_ms":3600000
}}

// Event subscriptions
{"id":"sub_1","method":"events.subscribe","params":{
  "subscriptions":[{"type":"pane.agent_status_changed","pane_id":"1-1","agent_status":"blocked"}]
}}

// Wait for agent state
herdr wait agent-status 1-1 --status done
herdr wait agent-status 1-1 --status blocked
```

**Semantic states:** `idle`, `working`, `blocked`, `done`, `unknown`
**custom_status:** visual-only label, doesn't affect waits/notifications/rollups
**Event types:** `pane.agent_status_changed`, `workspace.created/updated/renamed/closed/focused`

## 4. How Pi Could Use Herdr to Manage TUI Sessions Autonomously

**Agent Skill File** (`SKILL.md` at https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/blob/master/SKILL.md):

Install as a skill named `herdr` in Pi's skill system. Key guardrail: **`HERDR_ENV=1` must be set** — Pi is only active when running inside a Herdr pane.

**Capabilities with skill installed:**
- Inspect workspaces, tabs, panes, neighboring agents
- Split panes and run commands without stealing focus (`pane split`, `pane run`)
- Read pane output and recent logs (`pane read`)
- Wait for servers, tests, or another agent to finish (`wait agent-status`, `wait output`)
- Start helper agents in sibling panes (`agent start`)

**Autonomous orchestration pattern:**
```bash
# Pi spawns a reviewer agent in a split pane
herdr agent start reviewer --cwd ~/project --split right -- claude

# Pi waits for reviewer to finish
herdr wait agent-status reviewer --status done

# Pi reads reviewer output
herdr pane read reviewer --source recent --lines 120

# Pi reports its own state back
herdr pane report-agent "$HERDR_PANE_ID" --source herdr:pi --agent pi --state working --custom-status "integrating feedback"
```

**Programmatic autonomous loop via socket API:**
1. Pi opens Unix socket to `~/.config/herdr/herdr.sock`
2. Sends `pane.report_agent` to declare state transitions
3. Subscribes to `events.subscribe` for blocked/working changes on sibling panes
4. Uses `events.wait` for one-shot coordination ("wait until pane 1-2 is done")
5. Spawns new panes via `pane.split` + `pane.run` for parallel work
6. Reports `blocked` when Pi needs human approval (prevents Herdr from sending input)
7. Reports `done` when task complete, triggering notifications/rollups

**State rollup meaning:** Blocked pane → blocked tab → blocked workspace → Herdr UI highlights which project needs attention. Pi managing multiple TUI sessions means Herdr sidebar shows real-time status of all concurrent agent work.

## Evidence

- `https://herdr.dev/docs/integrations/` — Pi section, integration install/uninstall, lifecycle authority model
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/agents/` — supported agents table, status authority, detection manifests, state rollups
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/agent-skill/` — SKILL.md reference, `HERDR_ENV=1` guardrail, agent-facing commands
- `https://herdr.dev/docs/socket-api/` — Unix socket transport, `pane.report_agent`, `pane.report_agent_session`, `pane.report_metadata`, event subscriptions, waits, worktree methods

## Caveats

- Pi integration version 2+ required for native session restore
- Screen manifest detection without integration may miss some lifecycle transitions (hence integration preferred)
- `HERDR_ENV=1` check is critical — agent outside Herdr must not attempt socket control
- Socket API is newline-delimited JSON — not gRPC/REST, so custom client needed for programmatic use
- Local TOML manifest overrides at `~/.config/herdr/agent-detection/<agent>.toml` can customize detection behavior
- Blocked detection is deliberately strict — unusual new prompts may show as `idle` until Herdr learns that screen shape

## Goal
Fetch herdr SKILL.md, integration docs, and pi extension source code.

## Steps
- Step 1: Fetch SKILL.md from GitHub raw
- Step 2: Fetch herdr.dev integration docs
- Step 3: Fetch herdr repo for pi extension TypeScript source

Starting all three fetches now.Got SKILL.md and integration docs. Now hunting for the pi extension source code in the repo.Found `src/integration/assets/pi/`. Fetching the actual TS extension file now.Now let me also get the full SKILL.md (it was truncated earlier) and the pi-specific section from the integration docs.All three fetched successfully. Here's everything.

---

## Answer

All three resources located and extracted.

---

## Evidence

### 1. SKILL.md (full — 221 lines)

**Source:** `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/master/SKILL.md`

Key structure:

```yaml
---
name: herdr
description: "Control herdr from inside it. Manage workspaces and tabs, split panes, spawn agents, read output, and wait for state changes..."
---
```

**Prerequisite:** Check `HERDR_ENV=1`. If not set, stop — you're not inside herdr.

**Core concepts:**
- **Workspaces** — project contexts, each with tabs
- **Tabs** — subcontexts inside a workspace, each with panes
- **Panes** — terminal splits, each running its own process
- **Agent status** — `idle`, `working`, `blocked`, `done`, `unknown`
- **IDs** — compact, non-durable (e.g. `1-2` for pane). Re-read after close/split.

**Key commands:**

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `herdr pane list` | See all panes + which is focused |
| `herdr pane read 1-1 --source recent --lines 50` | Read another pane's output |
| `herdr pane split 1-2 --direction right --no-focus` | Split pane, keep focus |
| `herdr pane run "$NEW_PANE" "npm run dev"` | Run command in pane |
| `herdr pane send-text 1-1 "hello"` | Send text without Enter |
| `herdr pane send-keys 1-1 Enter` | Send keystroke |
| `herdr pane close 1-3` | Close a pane |
| `herdr wait output 1-3 --match "ready" --timeout 30000` | Block until text appears |
| `herdr wait agent-status 1-1 --status done --timeout 60000` | Block until agent done |
| `herdr workspace create --cwd /path --label "api"` | Create workspace |
| `herdr tab create --workspace 1 --label "logs"` | Create tab |

**Recipes include:** run server + wait, run tests separately, check sibling agent, watch pane robustly, spawn new agent, coordinate agents.

**Important notes:**
- `pane read` returns text, not JSON
- `--source recent-unwrapped` matches waiter text (soft wraps joined)
- `pane read --format ansi` for TUI feedback
- IDs compact on close — always re-read current IDs
- `workspace create` returns `result.workspace`, `result.tab`, `result.root_pane`
- `pane split` returns new pane id at `result.pane.pane_id`

---

### 2. herdr.dev Integration Docs — Pi Section

**Source:** `https://herdr.dev/docs/integrations/`

**Pi integration install:**
```bash
herdr integration install pi
```

**What it does:** Writes a bundled TypeScript extension to:
```
~/.pi/agent/extensions/herdr-agent-state.ts
```

If `PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR` is set, writes to `$PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR/extensions/herdr-agent-state.ts` instead.

**Requirements:** The extensions directory must already exist. Uninstall removes only that extension file.

**Other integrations documented:** OMP, Claude Code, Codex, GitHub Copilot CLI, Kimi Code CLI, Droid, OpenCode, Kilo Code CLI, Hermes Agent, Qoder CLI, Cursor Agent CLI — plus custom status labels and debug integration state sections.

---

### 3. Pi Integration Extension Source Code

**Source:** `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/master/src/integration/assets/pi/herdr-agent-state.ts`

**Rust embedding:** In `src/integration/mod.rs`:
```rust
const PI_EXTENSION_INSTALL_NAME: &str = "herdr-agent-state.ts";
const PI_EXTENSION_ASSET: &str = include_str!("assets/pi/herdr-agent-state.ts");
const PI_INTEGRATION_VERSION: u32 = 2;
const PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR_ENV_VAR: &str = "PI_CODING_AGENT_DIR";
```

**Full TypeScript extension (242 lines):**

The extension is a pi agent plugin that:

1. **Connects to herdr** via Unix socket using `HERDR_ENV`, `HERDR_SOCKET_PATH`, `HERDR_PANE_ID` env vars
2. **Reports agent state** (`working`/`blocked`/`idle`) to herdr over `pane.report_agent` RPC
3. **Tracks session identity** via `session_start` → extracts session file path + session ID from pi's `ctx.sessionManager`
4. **Handles lifecycle events:**
   - `session_start` → publish state + update session ref
   - `agent_start` → set `agentActive=true`, publish working
   - `agent_end` → check for retryable errors (429, 500, 502, 503, rate limits, etc.), hold for retry grace period (`retryGraceMs=2500ms`), else schedule idle (`idleDebounceMs=250ms`)
   - `session_shutdown` → `releaseAgent()` (sends `pane.release_agent`)
   - `herdr:blocked` custom event → increment/decrement blocked counter
5. **State machine** — `desiredState()` priority: blockedCount > failureBlocked > agentActive/retryHold > idle
6. **Dedup** — `publishState()` skips if state+message unchanged (unless `force=true`)
7. **Retry logic** — On retryable provider errors, holds `working` for `retryGraceMs` then transitions to `blocked`. Pattern matches: `overloaded`, `rate.?limit`, `429`, `5xx`, `timeout`, `connection.?error`, `fetch failed`, etc.
8. **Queue drain** — Serializes state reports to avoid concurrent socket writes
9. **Env overrides:**
   - `HERDR_PI_IDLE_DEBOUNCE_MS` (default 250)
   - `HERDR_PI_RETRY_GRACE_MS` (default 2500)

Key API calls in the extension:
```typescript
// Report state
{ method: "pane.report_agent", params: { pane_id, source: "herdr:pi", agent: "pi", state, message, seq } }

// Release agent on shutdown
{ method: "pane.release_agent", params: { pane_id, source: "herdr:pi", agent: "pi", seq } }
```

---

## Caveats

- GitHub API search for `herdr-agent-state` returned empty (auth required for code search). Found file via directory traversal instead.
- The `src/integration/mod.rs` is large (~4000+ lines) — only extracted pi-relevant sections.
- SKILL.md and TS extension were both truncated at 2000 output lines but full content was saved to temp files and read completely via `read` tool.

## Current State

| Component | Status |

## What You're Missing

Two things get installed:

### 1. Integration Extension (`herdr-agent-state.ts`)
Reports pi's state to herdr in real time. When pi is **working**, **blocked** (needs your approval), or **idle**, herdr's sidebar shows it. Also handles session restore if herdr restarts.

### 2. Skill (`SKILL.md`)
Gives pi the ability to **control herdr** — split panes, spawn agents in sibling panes, read their output, wait for them to finish. This is what enables autonomous orchestration.

## Setup

One command:
```bash
herdr integration install pi
```

This writes `~/.pi/agent/extensions/herdr-agent-state.ts`. The skill file pi already has access to herdr commands via CLI.

## What You Can Do After Setup

### Self-monitoring
Pi reports its own state → herdr sidebar shows real-time status across all your agent sessions.

### Debugging
Pi can read any pane's output:
```bash
herdr pane read <pane_id> --source recent --lines 50
```

### Autonomous terminal sessions
Pi can spawn and coordinate other agents:
```bash
# Split a pane and run a reviewer
herdr pane split 1-2 --direction right --no-focus
herdr pane run <new_pane_id> "claude"
herdr wait agent-status <new_pane_id> --status done
herdr pane read <new_pane_id> --source recent --lines 120
```

### Multi-agent orchestration
Pi in one pane, Claude in another, Kimi in a third — all coordinated via herdr's socket API. Herdr tracks which agent is blocked/working/done and rolls state up to workspace level.

content.text:
## Findings

### 1. Pi skills directory exists
```
~/.pi/agent/skills/
├── agents-md-writer/
│   └── SKILL.md
└── joelhooks-pi-tools-pi-tui-design/
    ├── SKILL.md
    └── references/examples.md
```

### 2. Skill structure pattern
Each skill = **directory** with a `SKILL.md` inside. Optional `references/`, `scripts/`, `assets/` subdirs. Directory name doesn't have to match skill `name:` in frontmatter (Pi relaxes this rule).

### 3. How Pi loads skills (from docs)

Key discovery rules:
- `~/.pi/agent/skills/` — directories with `SKILL.md` are **discovered recursively**
- At startup, Pi scans locations, extracts names/descriptions into system prompt
- Full `SKILL.md` loaded on-demand when agent matches a task

---

## Answer: Manual placement required

**Herdr does NOT handle this automatically.** Pi's skill loading is purely filesystem-based. The `SKILL.md` must exist at a path Pi scans. To add herdr skill:

```bash
# Option A: Download SKILL.md directly
mkdir -p ~/.pi/agent/skills/herdr
curl -o ~/.pi/agent/skills/herdr/SKILL.md https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ogulcancelik/herdr/master/SKILL.md

# Option B: Clone the whole repo if it has supporting files
git clone https://github.com/ogulcancelik/herdr ~/.pi/agent/skills/herdr
```

**Naming note:** Herdr's SKILL.md has frontmatter with `name` and `description`. The directory name (`herdr`) should match the skill name for clarity, though Pi allows mismatch.

**Also check:** `~/.agents/skills/` is another global scan location — would work there too.

## Scope
- filesToModify: []
- filesToCreate: ["~/.pi/agent/skills/herdr/SKILL.md"]
- changeType: "single-file"
- maxLinesPerFile: 200

## Recommendation
1. Check herdr's SKILL.md frontmatter to verify it has required `name` and `description` fields
2. Place it in `~/.pi/agent/skills/herdr/SKILL.md`
3. Restart pi session — skill will appear in available skills list
4. No herdr-specific integration needed; Pi treats all skills identically via filesystem discovery

herdr wait agent-status <new_pane> --status done

**Coordinate parallel work:**
```bash
herdr workspace create --cwd ~/project --label "api"
herdr agent start reviewer --cwd ~/project --split right -- claude
herdr wait agent-status reviewer --status done
```

content.text:
Here is the full contents of `/Users/shivam94/AGENTS.md`:

---

# Communication: Caveman Mode (Default)

**Always load caveman skill first** at session start. Skill contains full rules.

- Say "normal mode" or "stop caveman" to disable
- Default intensity: Full
- Think caveman too: short, efficient, no mental filler

---

# Model Selection — Harness-Based

## Default
DeepSeek Flash (`deepseek-v4-flash-2`) — cheap, fast, for all text tasks.

## Vision Tasks
Kimi K-2.6 (`kimi-k2.6-2`) — expensive, vision-capable. Use sparingly.

### How to handle images
1. Save image locally via `bash` tool if needed
2. Call mimo-v2.5-vision through Nyro:
   ```bash
   curl -s http://localhost:19530/v1/chat/completions \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"model": "mimo-v2.5-vision", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Describe this image"}, {"type": "image_url", "image_url": {"url": "data:image/png;base64,..."}}]}]}'
   ```
3. Present vision models's response
4. **Never ask user to switch models** — handle via API call

---

# Subagent Delegation — Use `delegate` Tool

**Use `delegate(specialist, task)` for complex, multi-step tasks.** Don't do everything in one context.

## When to delegate

| Task | Specialist |
|------|------------|
| Investigate unfamiliar codebase | `scout` |
| Implement a feature end-to-end | `coder` |
| Review code for bugs/security | `reviewer` |
| Research docs/configs/code | `researcher` |
| Create/edit documentation | `writer` |

## Rules

- **Simple tasks stay inline.** Read a file, make one edit, run one command — no delegation.
- **Sequential steps.** One delegate call at a time. Review the output. Then call the next.
- **Always pass context.** Delegates inherit nothing. Include file paths, error messages, constraints in the task description.
- **Synthesize results.** Don't just forward output — summarize, verify, act on it.
- **Default to inline first.** Only escalate to delegate when you'd burn >2 tool calls exploring or the task genuinely benefits from isolation.

---

# Philosophy: Always Test What You Claim

**Rule:** Test everything before claiming it works. No exceptions.

## What to test
- Code: run it, check errors, verify output
- Files: validate syntax, check paths
- CLI: execute, verify exit code
- Extensions: reload, verify tool appears

## Evidence format
State what you did → show output → pass/fail with confidence

---

# Anti-Slop Rules

**Don't create files unless asked.** No proposal files, no audit files, no summary files.

- Research: present findings in chat
- Proposals: discuss in chat, await approval
- Documentation: only when user explicitly requests

# Tool Rules

**Use `gh` for GitHub.** Not curl.
- `gh repo view` — repo info
- `gh issue list/view` — issues
- `gh release list/view` — releases
- `gh api` — raw API calls

# Web Search — Use `web-access` Skill

**Never curl/search manually. Always use the web-access plugin scripts.**

```bash
# Plugin path (resolve dynamically)
WEB_ACCESS=$(ls -d ~/.codex/plugins/cache/home-plugins/web-access/*/scripts/ 2>/dev/null | tail -1)

# Web search
python3 ${WEB_ACCESS}web_search.py "query here"

# Fetch URL content
python3 ${WEB_ACCESS}fetch_content.py "https://example.com"

# Code search
python3 ${WEB_ACCESS}code_search.py "React useEffect cleanup"
```

### Rules
- **Default provider**: Exa (zero-config, no API key needed)
- **Never** use raw `curl` to scrape search engines
- **Never** call the LLM API to "search the web"
- **Never** try to read skills as MCP resources
- Search then fetch: use `web_search` for discovery, `fetch_content` for deep dives

---

## Iterative Development — Build & Use Simultaneously

This orchestrator extension in pi is developed and used in parallel. Every session is a test of the workflow.

**Report bugs & difficulties.** When using the orchestrator pattern, note any friction:
- Does the delegation handoff work reliably?
- Are there false positives (blocking valid calls) or false negatives (allowing invalid ones)?
- Do subagent sessions initialize with the right tool sets?
- Any other issues or scope for improvement

**How to report.** After each task, include a "Session Feedback" section covering:
1. What worked
2. What didn't work (blocking issues)
3. What was friction (annoyable but workaroundable)
4. Specific reproduction steps if a bug

**Why.** Traditional testing catches known cases. Real usage catches edge cases
**Fix loop.** Bug found → report in session feedback → fix in next session → verify. No bug hit twice.

---

That's the complete file. Key takeaways:

- **Caveman mode** is default communication style (short, terse)
- **Model routing**: DeepSeek Flash for text, Kimi K2.6/vision for images
- **Subagent delegation** via `delegate(specialist, task)` with 5 specialist types
- **Test everything** before claiming it works
- **Anti-slop**: no unsolicited files, present findings in chat
- **Use `gh`** for GitHub, **web-access scripts** for web search
- **Iterative dev**: report session feedback after each task

## Session Feedback

### What didn't work (blocking)

### What was friction

### Reproduction steps

2. Call `delegate(coder, "run these commands: ...")` — blocked, wants scope from a NEW scout call
3. Even adding `## Scope` with empty arrays in scout task description doesn't satisfy it

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Here's the complete analysis:

---

## 1. "Scope required before coding" error — exact code

**File:** `/Users/shivam94/.pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator/delegate-tool.ts`, inside `execute()`:

```typescript
// === ADAPTIVE GATING: block coder without prior scope ===
if (params.specialist === "coder" && !_cachedScope) {
    return {
        content: [{
            type: "text" as const,
            text: `⛔ **Scope required before coding.**

No structured scope was found. This means either:
1. \`delegate(scout, ...)\` was not called first, or
2. Scout ran but its scope output couldn't be parsed (check debug logs at /tmp/pi-debug/)

The scout must output a \`## Scope\` section with \`filesToModify\` or \`filesToCreate\` arrays.

\`\`\`
delegate(scout, "analyze the auth system")
// scout must output:
// ## Scope
// - filesToModify: ["src/auth.ts"]
delegate(coder, "fix the token expiry")
\`\`\``
        }],
        details: {},
    } as any;
}
```

Guard condition: `params.specialist === "coder" && !_cachedScope`. Module-level variable `let _cachedScope: Scope | null = null;` starts null, reset to null on `before_agent_start`.

---

## 2. WHY this scope gate exists

**AGENTS.md says it plainly:**

> "Require scout before coder. Tool-level gate, not prompt-level. Prompts degrade."

**The problem it solves:**

Without scope, coder has full `read/write/edit/bash` access and no architectural understanding. It would start writing code based on the user's request alone — no investigation of existing patterns, no file mapping, no understanding of where changes should land. This leads to:

- **Wrong files modified** — coder guesses at file paths instead of tracing code
- **Architectural violations** — no awareness of existing patterns, naming conventions, module boundaries
- **Scope creep** — no predetermined boundary of what files are in/out of scope

The gate forces a **planned workflow**: scout investigates → produces structured scope → coder operates within that scope. The scope isn't just a file restriction — it's an architecture plan.

**Two enforcement layers:**

1. **Gate (delegate-tool.ts):** Blocks `delegate(coder, ...)` call entirely if no `_cachedScope` exists. LLM gets the block message and self-corrects by calling scout first.

2. **Scope-guard (scope-guard.ts):** Even after coder starts, individual `write`/`edit` tool calls are intercepted. Reads `.pi/scope.json` and blocks writes to files not in `filesToModify` or `filesToCreate`. Also enforces `maxLinesPerFile` in strict mode.

---

## 3. Scout output parsing — `extractScopeFromOutput()`

**File:** `delegate-tool.ts`, function `extractScopeFromOutput(output: string): Scope | null`

Two parsing strategies, tried in order:

### Strategy 1: JSON block
```typescript
const jsonMatch = output.match(/```(?:json)?\s*(\{[\s\S]*?(?:"filesToModify"|"filesToCreate")[\s\S]*?\})/);
```
Expects a fenced JSON block:
```json
{"filesToModify": ["src/auth.ts"], "filesToCreate": [], "maxLinesPerFile": 400}
```

### Strategy 2: `## Scope` section with key:value format
```typescript
const scopeSection = output.match(/## Scope\n([\s\S]*?)(?:\n##|$)/);
```
Expects this format inside the section:
```
## Scope
- filesToModify: ["src/auth.ts", "src/utils.ts"]
- filesToCreate: ["src/new.ts"]
- maxLinesPerFile: 400
```
Parses with regexes:
- `- filesToModify: [...entries...]`
- `- filesToCreate: [...entries...]`  
- `- maxLinesPerFile: <number>`

The `- ` prefix is optional, as is markdown bold wrapping (`*{0,2}`).

### Derived fields:
- `changeType`: `"single-file"` if total files ≤ 1, else `"multi-file"`
- `maxLinesPerFile`: defaults to 400 if not specified

### When it's called:
**After ANY subagent completes** (not just scout). This allows flexible workflows — researcher could also produce scope.

---

## 4. Bypass / flag for tasks without file changes

**No existing bypass.** The gate is absolute:

```typescript
if (params.specialist === "coder" && !_cachedScope) {
    return { /* BLOCKED */ };
}
```

However, there IS a workaround by design:

- **Use `researcher` or `writer` specialist instead of `coder`** — neither triggers the scope gate
- **`reviewer` and `scout`** are ALWAYS ALLOWED (read-only, no scope needed)

The `Scope` type has `changeType: "single-file" | "multi-file"` and `gateMode: "strict" | "relaxed"`:

```typescript
export type ScopeGateMode = "strict" | "relaxed";
// strict = multi-file change, full planning required
// relaxed = single-file change, lightweight scope
```

In **relaxed mode** (single-file), scope-guard skips `maxLinesPerFile` enforcement. But the initial gate still requires scope to exist.

**No `--no-scope` flag, no override, no opt-out.** AGENTS.md explicitly warns:

> "Don't weaken the gate to pass tests. The gate is the core mechanism."

---

## 5. Full orchestration flow

### Complete pipeline:

```
User prompt
  │
  ▼
[1] before_agent_start (index.ts)
  ├─ Strips all tools → only "delegate" available
  ├─ Generates plan steps from prompt keywords
  ├─ Sets up plan panel widget
  └─ Injects delegation instructions into system prompt
  │
  ▼
[2] LLM calls delegate(scout, "investigate ...")
  │
  ├─ delegate-tool.ts execute()
  │   ├─ Gate check: specialist=scout → not coder → PASS
  │   ├─ Plan panel: create/append step "Scout: investigate ..."
  │   └─ Calls runSubagent(scout, task, cwd, ...)
  │
  ├─ subagent-runner.ts runSubagent()
  │   ├─ writeScopeFile(cwd, null) → skipped (scout, no scope)
  │   ├─ Creates isolated AgentSession (only read + bash tools)
  │   ├─ session.prompt(task) → scout runs
  │   │   ├─ scout outputs ## Goal / ## Steps (parsed for activity feed)
  │   │   └─ scout outputs ## Scope section at end
  │   └─ Returns { output, turns }
  │
  ├─ Back in delegate-tool.ts execute()
  │   ├─ extractScopeFromOutput(result.output)
  │   ├─ If scope found → _cachedScope = extractedScope ✓
  │   ├─ completePlanStep()
  │   └─ Returns output to orchestrator LLM
  │
  ▼
[3] LLM calls delegate(coder, "implement ...")
  │
  ├─ delegate-tool.ts execute()
  │   ├─ Gate check: specialist=coder && !_cachedScope?
  │   │   └─ _cachedScope IS set → PASS ✓
  │   ├─ scopeToUse = _cachedScope (from scout)
  │   ├─ Calls runSubagent(coder, task, cwd, ..., scopeToUse)
  │
  ├─ subagent-runner.ts runSubagent()
  │   ├─ writeScopeFile(cwd, scopeToUse)
  │   │   └─ Writes .pi/scope.json with files + gateMode
  │   ├─ Creates isolated AgentSession (read + bash + edit + write)
  │   ├─ session.prompt(task) → coder runs
  │   │   ├─ Each write/edit call → scope-guard.ts intercepts
  │   │   │   ├─ Reads .pi/scope.json
  │   │   │   ├─ Checks path is in approved list
  │   │   │   ├─ Checks line count (strict mode only)
  │   │   │   └─ Blocks if out of scope
  │   │   └─ Coder completes
  │   └─ Returns { output, turns }
  │
  ├─ Back in delegate-tool.ts execute()
  │   ├─ extractScopeFromOutput(result.output) → may update cache
  │   ├─ completePlanStep()
  │   └─ Returns output to orchestrator LLM
  │
  ▼
[4] LLM calls delegate(reviewer, "review ...")
  ├─ Gate check: specialist=reviewer → not coder → PASS (always)
  ├─ No scope file written
  ├─ Read-only review
  └─ Returns output

[5] LLM synthesizes all outputs → final answer to user
```

### Scope lifecycle:

```
_scopedCache = null
  │
  ▼ scout runs → extractScopeFromOutput() → _cachedScope = { filesToModify, filesToCreate, changeType }
  │
  ▼ coder runs → scopeToUse = _cachedScope → writeScopeFile() → .pi/scope.json written
  │                     scope-guard enforces per-write
  │
  ▼ coder finishes → .pi/scope.json stays (not cleared)
  │                   _cachedScope stays (re-iteration allowed)
  │
  ▼ next session → before_agent_start → _cachedScope = null (reset)
```

### Key design decisions:

| Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|
| Tool-level gate (not prompt) | "Prompts degrade" — LLMs forget instructions over turns |
| Scope stays cached after coder | Allow re-iteration without re-calling scout |
| Any specialist can produce scope | Not just scout — researcher could also |
| `extractScopeFromOutput` called after ALL subagents | Flexible: any specialist output is checked |
| `gateMode` derived from `changeType` | Single-file = relaxed (no line limit), multi-file = strict |
| Self-correction, not crash | Block message teaches LLM what to do next turn |

content.text:
Good research. Now I see the full picture.

## The Intention

The scope gate exists because **coder has full write access and no architectural context**. Without investigation first, coder would:
- Guess at file paths
- Violate existing patterns
- Creep scope beyond what's needed

The gate forces: **investigate → scope → implement**. It's tool-level, not prompt-level, because "prompts degrade."

## The Friction

I was using coder to run `herdr integration install` — pure CLI commands, no file writes. That's a false positive. The scope gate is solving a real problem (preventing blind code changes) but I triggered it by misrouting the task.

## The Fix (Respecting Intention)

**I was using the wrong specialist.** Scout has `read + bash`. Running shell commands is exactly what bash does. I should have routed "run these 3 commands" to scout, not coder.

The workflow that works within the design:

```
# For CLI execution (no file changes):
delegate(scout, "run: herdr integration install codex, then claude, then hermes. Report output.")

# For code changes (file writes):
delegate(scout, "investigate the auth system")  → produces scope
delegate(coder, "fix the token expiry")         → scope gate passes
```

The real question: should there be an explicit specialist for "execute commands" that's separate from scout's "investigate" role? Or is scout's bash access sufficient for both?

What's your take — add an `executor` specialist, or keep using scout for command execution?

content.text:
The roster is built dynamically from `specialists.ts`. That's where the actual specialist definitions live. Let me read that.## Files Found
- `/Users/shivam94/.pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator/index.ts` — wiring hub, builds delegation prompt
- `/Users/shivam94/.pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator/specialists.ts` — specialist definitions

## Key Code

The LLM sees a **dynamic roster** built at runtime. `index.ts` lines ~47-54 loop `SPECIALISTS` and emit:

```
### Specialist roster:
  - **scout** — tools: read, bash Read-only codebase investigator...
  - **coder** — tools: read, bash, edit, write Implementation specialist...
  - **reviewer** — tools: read, bash Read-only code reviewer...
  - **researcher** — tools: read, bash Read-only research specialist...
  - **writer** — tools: read, write, edit Documentation specialist...
```

**What the orchestrator LLM reads about when to use each** (from `specialists.ts` description fields):

| Specialist | Description (in prompt) | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **scout** | Read-only codebase investigator. Uses grep/find to locate code, read to examine files. | Architecture discovery, bug investigation, code tracing |
| **coder** | Implementation specialist with full read/write access. Uses edit/write for file changes, bash for verification. | Implementing features, fixing bugs |
| **reviewer** | Read-only code reviewer. Checks for bugs, security issues, performance problems, and style violations. | Code review (Critical/Warnings/Suggestions) |
| **researcher** | Read-only research specialist. Reads docs, configs, and code to answer questions with evidence-based answers and source references. | Answering questions about codebase |
| **writer** | Documentation specialist with read/write access. Creates and edits markdown docs. | READMEs, API docs, project documentation |

**The workflow hint** the orchestrator LLM also sees (from `index.ts` delegation instructions):

```
1. Analyze the request
2. Call delegate(scout, "investigate ...") — read output
3. Call delegate(coder, "implement ... based on: [scout output]") — read output
4. Call delegate(reviewer, "review ... based on: [coder output]") — read output
5. Synthesize all results into final answer
```

## Dependencies
- `SPECIALISTS` dict in `specialists.ts` → `listSpecialists()` → iterated in `index.ts` to build prompt
- `TERSE_INSTRUCTION` and `ACTIVITY_FEED_INSTRUCTION` injected into every specialist's own system prompt (not the orchestrator's)
- `Specialist` type from `./types.ts`

## Scope
- filesToModify: []
- filesToCreate: []
- changeType: "single-file" (if editing description text in specialists.ts) or "multi-file" (if restructuring)
- maxLinesPerFile: 400

## Recommendation
All specialist descriptions live in `specialists.ts` as plain strings in `SPECIALISTS[name].description`. That's the single source of truth for what the orchestrator LLM reads. The workflow ordering hint (`scout → coder → reviewer`) is hardcoded in `index.ts` delegation instructions. To change when/how a specialist is used, edit the `description` field in `specialists.ts`. To change the suggested workflow order, edit the `delegationInstructions` template in `index.ts`.

content.text:
Current state: clean slate, 1 pane, socket live. Here are concrete ideas:

---

## Practical Herdr + Pi Ideas

### 1. Self-Debug Pane
Pi spawns a sibling pane running its own logs. When something goes wrong, it reads the log pane instead of guessing.

```bash
# Split pane, tail pi logs
herdr pane split w6540ba47015941-1 --direction down --no-focus
herdr pane run <new_pane> "tail -f ~/.config/herdr/herdr-server.log"

# Later, when debugging:
herdr pane read <log_pane> --source recent --lines 80
```

### 2. Parallel Agent Orchestration
Pi in one pane, Claude reviewing in another, Codex testing in a third. Pi waits for each to finish, reads their output, synthesizes.

```bash
# Spawn Claude reviewer in split
herdr pane split <my_pane> --direction right --no-focus
herdr pane run <review_pane> "claude --print 'review src/auth.ts for security issues'"

# Spawn Codex for tests
herdr pane split <my_pane> --direction down --no-focus  
herdr pane run <test_pane> "codex --approval-mode full-auto 'run test suite and fix failures'"

# Wait for both to finish
herdr wait agent-status <review_pane> --status done --timeout 120000
herdr wait agent-status <test_pane> --status done --timeout 120000

# Read results
herdr pane read <review_pane> --source recent --lines 100
herdr pane read <test_pane> --source recent --lines 100
```

### 3. Live Monitoring Dashboard
Pi creates a dedicated workspace just for monitoring. Separate from the working workspace.

```bash
# Create monitoring workspace
herdr workspace create --label "monitoring"

# In monitoring workspace: spawn panes for
# - herdr server logs
# - pi agent logs  
# - system resources (htop/btop)
# - git status watcher
```

### 4. Autonomous Build & Verify
Pi kicks off a build, waits for completion, verifies output, retries on failure.

```bash
# Run build
herdr pane run <build_pane> "npm run build 2>&1"

# Wait for it to finish
herdr wait output <build_pane> --match "Build completed\|Error\|FAILED" --timeout 300000

# Read output and decide
herdr pane read <build_pane> --source recent --lines 50
```

### 5. Session Detach/Reattach
Start work, detach, reattach later from SSH or another terminal. Process keeps running.

```bash
# Start pi working on something
herdr --session refactor

# Detach (ctrl+b q) — server keeps running

# Later, from anywhere:
herdr session attach refactor
```

### 6. Blocked Agent Recovery
Herdr shows when pi is blocked (needs approval). Pi can detect this and prompt the user.

```bash
# Check if any agent is blocked
herdr agent list --json | grep blocked

# If pi itself is blocked, send it input
herdr agent send pi "yes, proceed with the refactor"
```

---

### Most Useful Starting Point

**Idea 1 (self-debug)** and **Idea 2 (parallel agents)** give immediate value. Want me to set up a demo workspace with a few split panes so you can see it in action?

export function getCurrentDate(): string {
  const now = new Date();
  const pad = (n: number) => String(n).padStart(2, '0');
  const y = now.getFullYear();
  const mo = pad(now.getMonth() + 1);
  const d = pad(now.getDate());
  const h = pad(now.getHours());
  const mi = pad(now.getMinutes());
  const s = pad(now.getSeconds());
  return `${y}-${mo}-${d} ${h}:${mi}:${s}`;
}
```

arguments.task:
Deep code analysis of the orchestrator extension at /Users/shivam94/.pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator/. Read ALL files. I need to understand the following based on real test output:

## Problems Observed in Tests

### Problem 1: Plan title is raw user prompt
The plan panel showed:
```
Plan: ◆ investigate node version installed npm
Plan: ◆ create file called hello.ts tmp
```
These are truncated versions of the user's raw prompt. It reads awkwardly — "create file called hello.ts tmp" is not a natural title.

**Find**: Where is the plan title constructed? Is it the raw user prompt? Is there any summarization/title generation step?

### Problem 2: Step descriptions are terse/mechanical
Steps showed:
```
✓ Scout: Check tmp orchestrator-test directory exists
✓ Scout: Scan tmp orchestrator-test directory report  
✓ Coder: Create following files tmp orchestrator-test
✓ Scout: Run test `cd tmp orchestrator-test
```
These read like machine-generated fragments, not natural descriptions.

**Find**: Where are step descriptions generated? Are they extracted from the LLM's plan? Is there templating?

### Problem 3: Generic steps for investigation-only tasks
When the task was just "investigate node version", the plan showed:
```
○ Implement
○ Review
```
These are from the default workflow template and don't apply to investigation-only tasks.

**Find**: Where is the default step list (Scout→Implement→Review→Test) generated? Is it always the same regardless of task type?

### Problem 4: Activity feed parsing
**Find**: How does the activity feed work? What regex/parsing extracts step descriptions from subagent output?

### What to return for each problem:
- Exact file and line numbers
- The code that produces the problematic behavior
- Whether there's an existing mechanism to fix it or if new code is needed

content.text:
Let me read the truncated parts of the key files.Now let me check the remaining bits and `shortenLabel`:Full codebase read complete. Here's the deep analysis:

---

## Answer

### Problem 1: Plan title is raw user prompt

**File:** `plan-panel.ts`, line ~210 (`generatePlanFromPrompt`) called from `index.ts`, line ~36

**Code path:**
1. `index.ts:36` — `generatePlanFromPrompt(prompt)` called with raw `event.prompt`
2. `index.ts:37` — `setupPlanPanel(shortenLabel(prompt), steps, ctx)` passes raw prompt (only shortened)
3. `plan-panel.ts:210-236` — `generatePlanFromPrompt()` returns step labels only, never title
4. `plan-panel.ts:93` — `renderPlanLines()` renders `Plan: ◆ ${goalTrunc}` where `goal` = the `shortenLabel(prompt)` value

**The `shortenLabel` function** (`token-saver.ts:91-136`) strips filler words, punctuation, and numbers, caps at 5 words. But it's a crude regex pipeline — not summarization. Example: `"create file called hello.ts tmp"` → shortens to `"create file hello ts tmp"` — still raw and awkward.

**There is NO summarization or title generation step.** The plan title is always `shortenLabel(rawPrompt)`.

**Fix mechanism:** None exists. Need either:
- LLM-based title generation (expensive at plan time)
- Smarter heuristics (e.g., extract verb + object, drop path noise)
- Use `generatePlanFromPrompt` to synthesize a summary title (e.g., "Create hello.ts in tmp")

---

### Problem 2: Step descriptions are terse/mechanical

**File:** `delegate-tool.ts`, line ~150

**Code:**
```typescript
const specName = specialist.name.charAt(0).toUpperCase() + specialist.name.slice(1);
const stepLabel = `${specName}: ${shortenLabel(params.task)}`;
```

Then in `plan-panel.ts:253-280`, `startDelegationStep(stepLabel)` either relabels a pre-planned step or appends this label.

The step description is `"<Specialist>: <shortenLabel(task)>"` — a mechanical concatenation. `shortenLabel` uses regex word extraction, producing fragments like `"Check tmp orchestrator test directory exists"` (articles stripped, numbers removed, max 5 words).

**Substep descriptions** come from `activity-feed.ts:345-363`, `toolCallToSubstep()`:
```typescript
case "read": return `Reading ${normalizePath(input?.path || input?.file_path)}`;
case "bash": return `Running: ${(input?.command || "...").slice(0, 40)}`;
```
These are auto-generated from tool call params — mechanical but functional.

**Fix mechanism:** None exists. `shortenLabel` is pure regex, no semantic understanding. Would need prompt-aware summarization or at minimum smarter noun-phrase extraction.

---

### Problem 3: Generic steps for investigation-only tasks

**File:** `plan-panel.ts`, lines 210-236 (`generatePlanFromPrompt`)

**Code:**
```typescript
export function generatePlanFromPrompt(prompt: string): string[] {
    const p = prompt.toLowerCase();
    const steps: string[] = [];
    const has = (words: string[]) => words.some((w) => p.includes(w));

    const needsTest = has(["test", "verify", "validate", "check"]);
    const needsDocs = has(["document", "doc", "readme", "explain", "documentation", "comment"]);

    // ALWAYS include the standard 3-step workflow
    steps.push("Investigate");
    steps.push("Implement");
    steps.push("Review");

    if (needsTest) steps.push("Test");
    if (needsDocs) steps.push("Document");

    return steps;
}
```

**Hard-coded 3-step template.** "Investigate", "Implement", "Review" always present regardless of task type. A pure investigation task still shows "Implement" and "Review" as pending steps.

Keyword detection only *adds* steps (Test, Document) — never *removes* the default 3. No logic to detect "investigation-only" tasks and skip "Implement"/"Review".

**Fix mechanism:** Partial infrastructure exists — `startDelegationStep` in `plan-panel.ts:253-280` has a "Case 3" that appends new steps dynamically, and skips unused pre-planned steps. But the pre-planned steps are still shown as `○ Implement` / `○ Review` in the widget.

Would need:
- Detect investigation-only tasks (no "create", "fix", "implement", "write" keywords)
- Return only relevant steps from `generatePlanFromPrompt`
- Or: dynamically remove unstarted steps when orchestrator finishes without delegating to them

---

### Problem 4: Activity feed parsing

**File:** `activity-feed.ts`, lines 177-230 (`parseTextForFeed`)

**Parsing mechanism:** Incremental line-by-line parsing of subagent text output, called on each `text_delta` event.

**State machine:**
1. Accumulates text in `state.rawText` (capped at 10KB)
2. Splits into lines, processes complete lines only (skips last partial line)
3. State flags: `inGoalSection`, `inStepsSection` — toggled by `## Goal` / `## Steps` markdown headers

**Regex/parsing:**
- Section headers: `trimmed.match(/^##\s+Goal/i)` and `trimmed.match(/^##\s+Steps/i)` (lines 198-203)
- Goal: first non-empty line after `## Goal` header (line 211)
- Steps: `extractStepLabel()` (lines 157-167) extracts from:
  - Bullets: `trimmed.match(/^[-*•]\s+(.+)/)`
  - Numbers: `trimmed.match(/^\d+[.)]\s+(.+)/)`
- Goal fallback: if no `## Goal` header, first non-header line becomes goal (line 217)

**Substep generation:** NOT from text parsing. Comes from tool execution events in `subagent-runner.ts:178-207`:
- `tool_execution_start` → `toolCallToSubstep(toolName, args)` → `addSubstep(feed, label)`
- `tool_execution_end` → `completeLastSubstep(feed, outputPreview)`

**Step lifecycle:** `message_end` event → `completeCurrentStep(feed)` (line 172 in `subagent-runner.ts`)

**The plan steps shown in the activity feed come from the subagent's `## Goal` / `## Steps` output**, which is generated by `ACTIVITY_FEED_INSTRUCTION` in `specialists.ts:15-32` — the prompt template that forces every subagent to output `## Goal` + `## Steps` before tool calls. So the mechanical step descriptions originate from the LLM following this template.

---

## Summary Table

| Problem | Root Cause | File:Line | Fix Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw prompt as title | `shortenLabel(rawPrompt)` — regex strip, no summarization | `plan-panel.ts:210-236`, `index.ts:36-37` | Medium — needs smart title extraction or LLM call |
| Mechanical step descriptions | `"${specName}: ${shortenLabel(task)}"` — regex concatenation | `delegate-tool.ts:150-151` | Low — could use LLM summarization or better heuristics |
| Generic steps for investigation tasks | Hard-coded 3-step template, keyword detection only adds | `plan-panel.ts:210-236` | Low — add detection for investigation-only tasks, filter steps |
| Activity feed parsing | Markdown header parsing + bullet regex, substeps from tool events | `activity-feed.ts:177-230`, `subagent-runner.ts:178-207` | N/A — this is the working system, step labels flow from subagent LLM output |

## Caveats

- `shortenLabel` aggressively strips articles, numbers, and punctuation — explains why `"investigate node version installed npm"` becomes mangled fragments
- `generatePlanFromPrompt` is intentionally simple (no LLM call) to avoid latency at plan-creation time

content.text:
Let me grab the remaining content and check for the `DynamicBorder` and `BorderedLoader` patterns.Now let me check the full ctx.ui API surface:## Answer

No built-in "progress bar", "pipeline", or "task tracker" widget exists. SDK provides **composable primitives** — you build progress UI from them. Here's what's available and how it maps to improving the orchestrator plan panel.

---

## Available Widget/UI Primitives

### From `@earendil-works/pi-tui` (component library)

| Component | What it does |
|---|---|
| `Text` | Multi-line text with word wrap, `setText()` for live updates |
| `Box` | Container with padding + background color |
| `Container` | Vertical child layout, `addChild/removeChild/clear` |
| `Spacer` | Empty lines |
| `Markdown` | Markdown + syntax highlighting, `setText()` for updates |
| `SelectList` | Scrollable list with selection, search, scroll info |
| `SettingsList` | Toggle/settings UI with key-value cycling |
| `DynamicBorder` | Full-width horizontal border (accent-colored) |
| `Image` | Kitty/iTerm2/Ghostty image rendering |

**No** `Progress`, `Steps`, `Status`, `Pipeline`, or `TaskTracker` components exist.

### From `ctx.ui` (Extension UI context)

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `ctx.ui.setWidget(key, content, opts?)` | Persistent panel above/below editor. Accepts `string[]` or component factory |
| `ctx.ui.setStatus(key, text)` | Footer status badge (per-extension, keyed) |
| `ctx.ui.setFooter(factory)` | Replace entire footer with custom component |
| `ctx.ui.setHeader(factory)` | Replace header area |
| `ctx.ui.setPinnedHeader(factory)` | Sticky header at terminal top |
| `ctx.ui.setWorkingIndicator(opts?)` | Customize streaming spinner |
| `ctx.ui.setWorkingVisible(bool)` | Show/hide streaming indicator |
| `ctx.ui.custom<T>(factory, opts?)` | Full custom component with focus. Supports `overlay: true` + `OverlayOptions` |
| `ctx.ui.notify(msg, type)` | Toast notification |
| `ctx.ui.select(title, options)` | Simple dialog picker |
| `ctx.ui.editor(title, prefill)` | Multi-line text editor dialog |
| `ctx.ui.setTitle(title)` | Terminal window title |

---

## Best Existing Pattern for Plan Panel: `setWidget`

The **plan-mode extension** (`examples/extensions/plan-mode/index.ts`) is the closest reference. It uses exactly the approach you'd want:

```typescript
// Live progress widget above editor
ctx.ui.setWidget("plan-todos", lines);

// Also sets footer status
ctx.ui.setStatus("plan-mode", ctx.ui.theme.fg("accent", `📋 ${completed}/${todoItems.length}`));

// Clear widget when done
ctx.ui.setWidget("plan-todos", undefined);
```

**Key insight from plan-mode:** `setWidget` accepts a factory `(tui, theme) => Component` for dynamic rendering with caching and `invalidate()` support.

### Recommended pattern for orchestrator:

```typescript
ctx.ui.setWidget("orchestrator", (_tui, theme) => ({
  render(width) {
    return steps.map(step => {
      const icon = step.status === 'done' ? theme.fg("success", "✓") :
                   step.status === 'active' ? theme.fg("accent", "●") :
                   step.status === 'error' ? theme.fg("error", "✗") :
                   theme.fg("dim", "○");
      return truncateToWidth(` ${icon} ${step.label}`, width);
    });
  },
  invalidate() {}
}));
```

---

## Hooks for Real-Time UI Updates

### Event hooks (from `pi.on()`):

| Event | When | Use for |
|---|---|---|
| `session_start` | Session begins | Initialize widget, bind extensions |
| `agent_start` / `agent_end` | LLM processing starts/ends | Spinner, status transition |
| `turn_start` / `turn_end` | Each LLM turn | Increment step counters |
| `tool_execution_start` / `tool_execution_end` | Tool runs | Track tool-level progress |
| `tool_call` | Before tool runs | Block/modify, extract step info |
| `turn_end` | After assistant message | Parse `[DONE:n]` markers, update checkboxes |

### Live update pattern:

```typescript
// Inside extension:
let handle = ctx.ui.custom(myComponent, { overlay: true, overlayOptions: { anchor: "right-center", width: 40 } });

// When state changes:
handle.requestRender(); // triggers re-render without closing
```

### Overlay for persistent side panel:

```typescript
ctx.ui.custom((tui, theme, _kb, done) => myProgressPanel, {
  overlay: true,
  overlayOptions: {
    anchor: "right-center",
    width: "25%",
    minWidth: 30,
    margin: { right: 1 },
    visible: (termWidth) => termWidth >= 100,  // responsive
  },
  onHandle: (h) => { /* h.focus(), h.setHidden(), h.unfocus() */ }
});
```

---

## What Doesn't Exist (Gaps for Improvement)

1. **No `BorderedLoader` for multi-step operations** — only a simple spinner+abort pattern exists
2. **No progress bar component** — you'd render one manually with Unicode blocks (`█░`) in a `Text` component
3. **No "steps" or "pipeline" widget** — plan-mode builds this ad-hoc from checkbox characters
4. **No `renderCall`/`renderResult` for plan steps** — only for tools. Plan progress must be built from scratch
5. **`setWidget` is limited to above/below editor** — no side panel placement (use `custom()` with `overlay: true` for that)

---

## Recommendations for Orchestrator Plan Panel

1. **Use `setWidget` + `setStatus`** for the plan checklist above editor (matches plan-mode pattern)
2. **Use `custom()` with `overlay: true` + `anchor: "right-center"`** for a persistent side panel showing detailed step state
3. **Build a custom component** extending `Container` with `Text` children — use `setText()` for live updates, `invalidate()` on theme changes
4. **Parse `[DONE:n]` markers** from assistant messages (plan-mode pattern) for step completion tracking
5. **Use `handle.requestRender()`** after state changes for re-render without re-mounting
6. **Cache rendered lines** with `cachedWidth`/`cachedLines` pattern for performance
7. **Use `DynamicBorder`** for visual separation between steps
8. **Use theme colors**: `success` (done), `accent` (active), `error` (failed), `dim` (pending), `muted` (skipped)

---

## Evidence

- TUI docs: `/Users/shivam94/.hermes/node/lib/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs/tui.md` — all patterns and components
- SDK docs: `/Users/shivam94/.hermes/node/lib/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs/sdk.md` — session.subscribe events
- Type defs: `/Users/shivam94/.hermes/node/lib/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/extensions/types.d.ts` — full `ExtensionUIContext` interface
- Plan-mode example: `examples/extensions/plan-mode/index.ts` — `setWidget` + `setStatus` + `[DONE:n]` parsing
- Todo example: `examples/extensions/todo.ts` — `Text` checkbox rendering pattern
- Status-line example: `examples/extensions/status-line.ts` — `setStatus` with turn tracking
- Widget-placement example: `examples/extensions/widget-placement.ts` — `setWidget` with `belowEditor`
- Overlay-qa-tests: `examples/extensions/overlay-qa-tests.ts` — overlay positioning, focus, animation, streaming
- Border-status-editor: `examples/extensions/border-status-editor.ts` — spinner in editor borders, `fitBorder` utility
- Working-indicator: `examples/extensions/working-indicator.ts` — animated spinner customization

## Caveats

- `setWidget` renders above/below editor only — no side panel (must use `custom()` overlay for that)
- Widget content is `string[]` or simple `Component` — no virtual scrolling built in for large lists
- `overlayOptions` uses percentages as strings (`"25%"`) not raw numbers for responsive sizing
- `handle.requestRender()` only works while overlay/custom component is alive — stale handles are disposed
- No built-in debouncing for rapid state updates — component-level caching handles this

---

## SITREP: Orchestrator Extension Audit

### Test Results

| Test | Task | Steps | Result | Time |
|------|------|-------|--------|------|
| #1 | "investigate node version" | 3 (investigate only) | ✅ Pass | 27s |
| #2 | "create hello.ts + test" | 4 (scout→coder→scout) | ✅ Pass | 1m 53s |

**Core functionality works.** Scout→coder delegation, scope gating, file creation, test verification — all operational.

---

### UI/UX Issues Found

#### Issue 1: Plan title is raw user prompt (HIGH)
```
Plan: ◆ investigate node version installed npm
Plan: ◆ create file called hello.ts tmp
```
**Root cause:** `shortenLabel(rawPrompt)` in `index.ts:37` → regex strip, no summarization. The `shortenLabel` function (token-saver.ts:91-136) just removes articles/numbers/punctuation and caps at 5 words. No semantic understanding.

**Fix:** Either LLM-generated title (adds latency) or smarter heuristics (extract verb + object, drop noise). Example: "create file called hello.ts tmp" → "Create hello.ts"

#### Issue 2: Generic steps for investigation-only tasks (MEDIUM)
```
Plan: ◆ investigate node version installed npm  ● 1/3
  ✓ Scout: Investigate current Node.js npm environment.
  ○ Implement    ← never happens
  ○ Review       ← never happens
```
**Root cause:** `generatePlanFromPrompt()` in `plan-panel.ts:210-236` always includes "Investigate", "Implement", "Review". Keyword detection only *adds* steps (Test, Document), never *removes* defaults.

**Fix:** Add detection for investigation-only tasks (no "create/fix/implement/write" keywords). Return only relevant steps.

#### Issue 3: Step descriptions are mechanical (LOW)
```
✓ Scout: Check tmp orchestrator-test directory exists
✓ Scout: Scan tmp orchestrator-test directory report
✓ Coder: Create following files tmp orchestrator-test
```
**Root cause:** `"${specName}: ${shortenLabel(task)}"` in `delegate-tool.ts:150-151`. Pure regex concatenation.

**Fix:** Minor — these are readable enough. Could improve with noun-phrase extraction but low priority.

#### Issue 4: Plan steps don't match actual execution (MEDIUM)
Pre-planned steps ("Implement", "Review") get relabeled by `startDelegationStep` when delegations arrive, but if the orchestrator never delegates to them, they stay as `○ Implement` / `○ Review` — showing phantom steps.

**Fix:** Dynamically remove unstarted steps when orchestrator finishes without using them, or don't pre-plan them.

---

### Code Locations

| Issue | File | Lines | Fix Complexity |
|-------|------|-------|----------------|
| Plan title | `index.ts` | 36-37 | Medium |
| Plan title | `plan-panel.ts` | 93, 210-236 | Medium |
| Generic steps | `plan-panel.ts` | 210-236 | Low |
| Step labels | `delegate-tool.ts` | 150-151 | Low |
| Phantom steps | `plan-panel.ts` | 253-280 | Low |

---

### SDK Recommendations

Current plan panel uses `setWidget` (above/better editor). Better options available:

| Approach | When to use |
|----------|-------------|
| `setWidget` + `setStatus` | Current approach, works fine for simple checklists |
| `custom()` + `overlay: true` | Side panel for detailed step state (right anchor) |
| `DynamicBorder` | Visual separation between steps |
| `[DONE:n]` markers | Parse from assistant messages for step completion (plan-mode pattern) |

---

### Priority Fixes

1. **Plan title** — most visible UX issue. Fix `shortenLabel` or add title summarization.
2. **Phantom steps** — "Implement"/"Review" showing for investigation tasks. Fix `generatePlanFromPrompt`.
3. **Step descriptions** — nice to have, low priority.

```

Same `setWidget` approach, just better content:

**Changes:**

- Labels: cleaner, no path fragments

## Fix 2: Side Panel Overlay (via `custom()` + `overlay: true`)

Instead of above-editor widget, persistent right-side panel:

```
┌─ Orchestrator ──────────────┐
│ ◆ Create hello.ts w/ tests  │
│                              │
│ ✓ Scout  Check directory    │
│ ✓ Scout  Scan files         │
│ ✓ Coder  Create files       │
│ ⠼ Scout  Run tests          │
│ ○                            │
│                              │
│ ● 3/4    1m 15s             │
└──────────────────────────────┘
```

**vs current (above editor):**
```
Plan: ◆ create file called hello.ts tmp  ● 3/4  1m 15s
  ✓ Scout: Check tmp orchestrator-test directory exists
  ✓ Scout: Scan tmp orchestrator-test directory report
  ✓ Coder: Create following files tmp orchestrator-test
  ⠼ Scout: Run test `cd tmp orchestrator-test
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
<editor content here>
```

**Tradeoffs:**

| | Current (setWidget) | Side Panel (overlay) |
|---|---|---|
| **Placement** | Above editor | Right side, fixed |
| **Editor space** | Compressed vertically | Full width preserved |
| **Visibility** | Scrolls away if output long | Always visible |
| **Responsiveness** | Works at any width | Needs ≥100 cols |
| **Complexity** | Simple `setWidget` | `custom()` + overlay API |
| **Best for** | Short tasks (2-4 steps) | Long orchestrations (5+ steps) |

Lightest touch — keep widget, add footer status:

  ✓ Scout  Check directory

────────────────────────────────────────────
 🔧 Orchestrator │ 3/4 steps │ 1m 15s       ← status badge (footer)
```

Uses `ctx.ui.setStatus("orchestrator", ...)` for persistent footer summary.

---

## Recommendation

**Start with Fix 1** (smart title + step filtering) — same rendering code, just better input data. Low effort, high impact.

**Then Fix 3** (add status badge) — one extra `setStatus` call, gives persistent visibility.

**Defer Fix 2** (side panel) — more complex, better for later when orchestrations get longer.

content.text:
Let me get the truncated portions.Now let me check how orchestrator consumes subagent results in the main orchestrator file.## Answer

### What the orchestrator (main agent) sees from subagents

**Tool return value only.** After `runSubagent()` completes, the orchestrator gets back:

```ts
{ output: string, turns: number }
```

That `output` is:
1. The subagent's raw text output, accumulated token-by-token via `session.subscribe` `text_delta` events
2. Compressed (ANSI stripped, blank-collapsed) via `compressOutput()`
3. Capped at 30,000 chars (`OUTPUT_CAP`)
4. Appended with `[Orchestrator Warnings]` if step completion count mismatches

The orchestrator then:
- Passes `result.output` as the tool result content back into its own context: `{ content: [{ type: "text", text: result.output }] }`
- Tries to extract `Scope` from the output (`extractScopeFromOutput`) — looking for `## Scope` section or JSON block with `filesToModify`/`filesToCreate`
- Caches scope for subsequent `coder` delegation

**Key insight: the orchestrator sees the FINAL output only.** All intermediate tool calls, spinners, progress — those are for UI rendering only. The orchestrator LLM never sees "Scout read 15 files, ran 3 grep commands." It only sees the subagent's final text response.

### What the user currently sees from subagents

**Three UI layers, all from intermediate events:**

| Layer | What | Source | Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plan Panel (widget)** | `◆ goal ● N/N ✓ done ⠋ active ○ pending` + substep detail lines | `plan-panel.ts` via `updatePlanStepDetail()` | Above editor (TUI widget) |
| **Chat Feed (Layer 2)** | Box-drawn task + `●N/N` progress + step/substep list with spinners | `activity-feed.ts` via `renderActivityFeed()` / `renderCombinedProgress()` | Inline in chat scroll |
| **Tool Result (final)** | Compressed output text | `result.output` in `delegate-tool.ts` `execute()` return | Chat message after tool completes |

The user sees real-time updates every 80ms (spinner animation) + on every tool start/end event. Substeps show `✓ Reading extension.ts → file not found` style lines.

**What's shown to user but NOT to orchestrator:**
- Tool call names and arguments (`event.toolName`, `event.args`)
- Per-tool output previews (first 80 chars of each tool result, `compressOutput` + truncated)
- Step completion timing (`startTime`/`endTime` per step/substep)
- Lint-guard custom messages from subagent

### The gap

| Dimension | Orchestrator sees | User sees | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intermediate steps** | Nothing — only final output | Real-time tool calls, spinners, substeps | Orchestrator blind to process |
| **Tool outputs** | Only final subagent text (capped 30K) | Per-tool output previews (80 chars each) | Orchestrator gets no per-tool data |
| **Scope extraction** | Parsed from final output text | Not shown to user at all | User unaware scope was extracted |
| **Step timing** | Not available | Per-step and per-substep durations | No performance feedback to orchestrator |
| **Lint messages** | Not forwarded to orchestrator | Shown via `onUpdate` with `status: "lint"` | Lint issues invisible to orchestrator LLM |
| **Error handling** | Gets `[error] msg` as output text | Gets error in feed + plan panel error state | Partial — orchestrator doesn't know *which step* failed |
| **Progress percentage** | Has `turns` count only | Has `completed/total` steps + substep counts | Orchestrator can't gauge completion % |

### Biggest gaps for vision doc

1. **Orchestrator is stateless across subagent calls.** It can't say "the scout read 14 files but only found 2 relevant ones" — it only gets the final summary. All intermediate intelligence is discarded.

2. **Scope extraction is fragile regex.** Falls back to markdown `## Scope` section parsing if JSON fails. If subagent doesn't format perfectly, scope is lost and coder is blocked.

3. **No feedback loop.** Orchestrator can't adjust strategy mid-delegation. It fires `delegate(scout, task)` and waits. No streaming of findings back. No ability to say "scout, look deeper in X."

4. **User sees more than orchestrator.** User gets rich real-time visibility (substeps, tool previews, timing) but orchestrator LLM gets a flat text blob. Information asymmetry goes the wrong direction.

## Evidence

- `specialists.ts`: All 5 specialists get `ACTIVITY_FEED_INSTRUCTION` forcing `## Goal` / `## Steps` output format. This is parsed by `parseTextForFeed()` to create UI progress — but orchestrator never sees this parsed structure.
- `subagent-runner.ts:127-130`: `session.subscribe` captures `text_delta` → `parseTextForFeed()` for UI only. Accumulates `output` string for orchestrator return.
- `subagent-runner.ts:267-273`: Output compressed + capped at 30K chars before returning.
- `delegate-tool.ts:167-178`: Orchestrator gets `{ content: [{ type: "text", text: result.output }] }` — just the compressed final text.
- `delegate-tool.ts:42-104`: `extractScopeFromOutput()` tries JSON then markdown parsing — the only structured data extraction from subagent output.
- `activity-feed.ts:259-290`: `toolCallToSubstep()` generates labels like "Reading extension.ts", "Running: grep auth" — visible to user, invisible to orchestrator.
- `plan-panel.ts:339-354`: `updatePlanStepDetail()` pushes substep history to widget — user-only.
- `subagent-runner.ts:163-167`: Lint-guard messages forwarded via `onUpdate` with `status: "lint"` — user sees them, orchestrator doesn't.

## Caveats

- `orchestrator.ts` (main file) doesn't exist as standalone — `delegate-tool.ts` is the effective entry point. Could be more orchestration logic in `index.ts` not examined.
- `MAX_FEED_STEPS = 6` and `MAX_FEED_SUBSTEPS = 8` cap what user sees — could be missing steps in long-running subagents.
- `OUTPUT_CAP = 30K` means orchestrator could lose significant output from research-heavy subagents.
- `renderCombinedProgress` intentionally omits `outputPreview` from chat feed (comment on line ~147) — user sees substep labels but not tool outputs in the chat widget view (only in plan panel detail lines).
